coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposites supporting environmental sustainability: Organic dye removal and bacterial inactivation

coredata.dc:description: Various studies are being carried out on the removal of organic dyes and the production of antibacterial agents. In this study, boron-doped carbon quantum dot (BCQD) was synthesized by hydrothermal method, and BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite structure was synthesized using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as the support material. The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite against MO, RhB dyes, and Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria was investigated. The surface, morphology, molecular, and crystal properties of BCQD@g-C3N4 were investigated using characterization methods such as Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). As a result of TEM analysis, it was determined that the average particle size of BCQD was 5.1 ± 1.14 nm and showed a homogeneous distribution on 2D g-C3N4. In the XRD spectrum for BCQDs, the diffraction peak corresponding to the (002) amorphous carbon phase was observed at 21.65° In the PL spectrum of B-CQD@g-C3N4s, the emission value was observed at 458 nm. In the study conducted by taking advantage of the photocatalytic feature of BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite, Rhodamine B (RhB) and Methyl orange (MO) were degraded by 65.58 % and 73.56 %, respectively, at the end of 120 min. Additionally, BCQD@g-C3N4 photocatalyst completely inhibited the growth of E. coli bacteria, which are frequently encountered in wastewater, at 90 minutes under sunlight. Escherichia coli (E. coli), which is frequently encountered in wastewater, BCQD@g-C3N4 completely prevented bacterial growth in the 90th minute under sunlight.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposites supporting environmental sustainability: Organic dye removal and bacterial inactivation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sen Research Group, Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Arts and Science, Dumlupinar University, Evliya Celebi Campus, 43100 Kutahya, Turkiye
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sen Research Group, Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Arts and Science, Dumlupinar University, Evliya Celebi Campus, 43100 Kutahya, Turkiye

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various studies are being carried out on the removal of organic dyes and the production of antibacterial agents. In this study, boron-doped carbon quantum dot (BCQD) was synthesized by hydrothermal method, and BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite structure was synthesized using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as the support material. The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite against MO, RhB dyes, and Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria was investigated. The surface, morphology, molecular, and crystal properties of BCQD@g-C3N4 were investigated using characterization methods such as Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). As a result of TEM analysis, it was determined that the average particle size of BCQD was 5.1 ± 1.14 nm and showed a homogeneous distribution on 2D g-C3N4. In the XRD spectrum for BCQDs, the diffraction peak corresponding to the (002) amorphous carbon phase was observed at 21.65° In the PL spectrum of B-CQD@g-C3N4s, the emission value was observed at 458 nm. In the study conducted by taking advantage of the photocatalytic feature of BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite, Rhodamine B (RhB) and Methyl orange (MO) were degraded by 65.58 % and 73.56 %, respectively, at the end of 120 min. Additionally, BCQD@g-C3N4 photocatalyst completely inhibited the growth of E. coli bacteria, which are frequently encountered in wastewater, at 90 minutes under sunlight. Escherichia coli (E. coli), which is frequently encountered in wastewater, BCQD@g-C3N4 completely prevented bacterial growth in the 90th minute under sunlight.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrialization, growing urbanization, increasing population, widespread use of unsustainable resources, and uncontrolled exploitation can cause irreparable damage to the environment (Kocak et al., 2023; Aygun et al., 2023). Although environmental pollution in this context is not a new concept, it continues to be a huge problem. It poses a great risk to humanity, causing various diseases and deaths (Arsalani et al., 2018). One of the biggest dangers of environmental pollution is water pollution resulting from various industrial and anthropogenic activities (Nasiri et al., 2023). Wastewater threatens lakes, rivers, oceans, and underground water resources (Algethami et al., 2023; Gusain et al., 2019). Textile, paper, pulp, chemical, fertilizer, pesticide, metal plating, battery, food processing, refinery, and pharmaceutical industries discharge millions of gallons of wastewater every day, polluting the terrestrial soils and water bodies around us (Gusain et al., 2019; Rashtizadeh et al., 2023). These wastewaters contain high concentrations of salts, dyes, heavy metals, oil emulsions, and microorganisms (Zhang et al., 2022). Organic dyes are molecules with complex structures that give color to fabrics and other materials. These dyes are widely used in the cosmetic, plastic, food, paper, and textile industries. This widespread use leads to the generation of large amounts of coloured wastewater. The World Bank estimates that 17 %−20 % of industrial water pollution comes from textile dyeing and refining (Mapazi et al., 2023). Many organic dyes are particularly resistant to heat or detergents. In this context, the removal of organic dyes from wastewater is of great importance due to the wide application areas of organic pollutants and their subsequent discharge to water bodies and terrestrial areas, their long-term persistence, strong resistance, and significant effects on human health and the environment (Gusain et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023; Dariani et al., 2016). In particular, the mixing of azo dyes into water prevents the penetration of sunlight into the water and damages the photochemical and biological activities of aquatic life. Additionally, many azo dyes are known to exhibit toxic and mutagenic properties. At this point, the use of water contaminated with dyes harms the environment and health. It has been reported that it can affect the liver, brain, and central nervous system in humans (Gusain et al., 2019; Bhatnagar and Jain, 2005; Kim et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2015; Waghchaure et al., 2022; da Silva et al., 2014). Many technologies have been developed for water and wastewater treatment, from physical treatment to chemical and biological treatment (Rashid et al., 2021; Bingül Reçber et al., 2022; Yildiz et al., 2023; Bayat et al., 2022; Nasiri and Arsalani, 2018). Of these technologies; Most of the existing physicochemical and biological treatment methods, such as carbon adsorption, flocculation, ozonation, and activated sludge processes, completely destroy paint contaminants, but they bring drawbacks such as slow operation, the need for expensive equipment and cause secondary pollution (Kim et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2015). At this point, studies aim to accelerate waste removal by focusing on effective and inexpensive methods.

In recent years, photocatalysis has attracted great attention as an alternative to organic pollutant degradation and decolorization methods (Azhar et al., 2023). This method differs from traditional chemical and biological methods with its various advantages such as the use of a visible region or UV light source, the use of a simple reactor, the absence of secondary pollution caused by decomposed organic substances, and the ability to be reprocessed in an environmentally friendly way. While photocatalysts are used in advanced dye removal with their high oxidation capacity, they are also used in many areas such as organic pollutant mineralization, CO2 reduction, and hydrogen production.

Nanomaterials are of great importance due to their excellent properties. Nanomaterials find their place in a wide range of application areas such as anticancer, antibacterial, photocatalyst, sensor, electronics, energy, fuel cell, food industries, Farasati Far et al. (2024), Javad Javid-Naderi et al. (2023), Pourmadadi et al. (2024), Fatima et al. (2024), El-Kady et al. (2023), Mekuye and Abera (2023). Carbon nitrides are long-chain structures containing carbon and nitrogen. g-C3N4 is a potential material to solve wastewater problems through photocatalysis due to its hydrothermal stability, the advantageous energy gap of 2.7 eV, and sunlight-driven photochemical performance (Rashtizadeh et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2022). Limitations that prevent the practical use of pure g-C3N4 include low sunlight absorption, higher risk of harmful charge recombination, limited specific area, and low efficiency (Azhar et al., 2023; He et al., 2022a). Today, various methods such as doping and passivation layer application, changing size and morphology, and nanocomposite development are frequently used to solve such problems related to C3N4. CQDs are a member of the carbon family with a diameter of <10 nm and have attracted much attention due to their superior properties such as tuneable optical properties, high chemical stability, good photostability, and low toxicity (Yashwanth et al., 2020). Furthermore, CQDs find a place in photocatalytic activity studies due to their photoluminescence (UCPL) properties, wide range of sensitivity to visible light, and low cost (Wang et al., 2019). In addition, new energy states resulting from different electronic configurations of CQDs doped with heteroatoms such as B, N, S, P, and Se can be formed and their physicochemical and catalytic properties can be improved (Yashwanth et al., 2020; Ozyurt et al., 2023). The addition of CQDs to g-C3N4s can expand the light-harvesting ability and efficiently transfer and store photogenerated electrons (Preeyanghaa et al., 2022).

In this work, we prepared BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite using the hydrothermal method, and the obtained nanocomposite was determined using different characterization methods. According to the literature, CQDs have a wide range of visible light sensitivity. In addition, g-C3N4 is a newly developed photocatalyst that has a layered structure similar to graphene. It is an excellent option for antibacterial applications due to its many impressive advantages, which include low cost, high chemical stability, outstanding biocompatibility, and facile synthesis. Due to all these features, we tried to examine the photocatalytic activity of BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite against MO, RhB dyes, and E. coli.

Citric acid (C6H6O7, 99 %), boric acid (H3BO3, ≥98 %), urea (NH2CONH2, 99.5 %), RhB (C28H31ClN2O3, 95 %), and MO (C14H14N3NaO3S, 85 %) chemicals were purchased in analytical purity and used without any purification.

The synthesis of BCQD@g-C3N4 photocatalyst was carried out in three steps. BCQD synthesis was carried out in the first stage of photocatalyst synthesis. In this context, the firstly, 3 g of citric acid and 1 g of boric acid were added to 50 mL dH2O (Elga Micra) and mixed using a magnetic stirrer (ISOLAB) until a homogeneous mixture was obtained. The homogeneous mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and kept at 180 °C for 6 h. After 6 h, it was observed that the white liquid solution turned into a dark brown solution. Then, the solution cooled to room temperature was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and centrifuged (SIGMA, 3–30 KS) at 10.000 rpm for 15 min. BCQDs were obtained and dried in the vacuum oven (BIOBASE BOV V50) at 80 °C (Phang et al., 2022).

In the second stage of the synthesis step, g-C3N4 was synthesized. 10 g of urea was transferred to a porcelain crucible, and g-C3N4 synthesis was carried out by covering the crucible and heating it in a muffle furnace at 550 °C (flow rate: 2 °C min−1) for 4 h. The resulting powder was crushed in a mortar (Paul et al., 2019).

In the last stage of the synthesis step, BCQD@g-C3N4 composite structure was obtained. At this stage, 10 mg BCQD in 5 mL dH2O and 50 mg of g-C3N4 in 20 mL dH2O were mixed separately in a magnetic stirrer for 30 min. Finally, both mixtures were subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. Then, both solutions were mixed together and kept in a magnetic stirrer for 30 min. The mixture, which turned beige in color, was added to the Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 150 °C for 4 h (Schema 1 ). The mixture cooled to room temperature was filtered to remove impurities and washed 3 times with dH2O. Finally, the hydrothermally obtained BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite structure was dried at 80 °C (Phang et al., 2022).

For photocatalytic studies, 100 mL of MO and RhB dye solutions were prepared at a concentration of 1 g/L. Then, 10 mg BCQD@g-C3N4 was added to the dye solutions and mixed for 30 min in the dark to ensure the adsorption-desorption balance between photocatalysis and dye. Following this process, the first measurement was taken on the Ultraviolet and visible light (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer and determined the initial absorption value. Immediately afterward, in order to determine the photocatalytic effect of BCQD@g-C3N4 on MO and RhB dyes, the dye solutions were kept under sunlight, and UV–Vis measurements were made every 30 min (Ameen et al., 2023). 400 W Halogen lamp was used as the light source.

The photocatalytic activity of BCQD@g-C3N4 against E. coli bacteria was performed under sunlight. E. coli bacteria were incubated in 100 mL nutrient broth at 37 °C for 24 h. After 24 h, centrifugation was performed to collect E. coli cells, and the pellet was washed 2 times with Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). E. coli cell pellets were then resuspended in 100 mL of saline (1 % NaCl). 50 mg BCQD@g-C3N4 was added to the E. coli suspension and gently mixed for 10 min in the dark. After keeping it in the dark, 100 μL of the bacterial solution was taken and planted in a petri dish. The bacterial solution was kept under sunlight, and 100 μL of the sample was seeded onto the agar plate at 30-min intervals, and all plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h (Ameen et al., 2023).

Photoluminescence (PL) studies of g-C3N4 and BCQD@g-C3N4, which have semiconductor structures, are shown in Fig. 1 a. In the 400–500 nm spectrum range, g-C3N4 and BCQD@g-C3N4 were observed to give high emissions at approximately 458 nm. As seen in Fig. 1a, g-C3N4 appears to have a higher emission peak than BCQD@g-C3N4. The lower emission of BCQD@g-C3N4 indicates the presence of B-doped CQD or suppressed electron-hole recombination. The obtained PL spectrum results show that CQDs play a role in photocatalytic activity by causing electron transport (Phang et al., 2022). In the PL spectrum of B-CQDs, the emission value was observed at 446 nm (λex:370 nm) (Figure S1a). To evaluate the fluorescence stability of B-CQD@g-C3N4s, their effects at different pHs were examined (Figure S2). It was observed that the FL of B-CQD@g-C3N4s increased from pH 2.0 to 7.0 and then decreased at alkaline pH values. It was determined that pH7 had the best FL value. The crystal structure and molecular structures were examined by XRD and FTIR. The molecular structures and chemical composition of g-C3N4 and BCQD@g-C3N4 were investigated using FTIR (Fig. 1b). As seen in Fig. 1b, the broad peak at 3202 cm−1 of g-C3N4 and BCQD@g-C3N4 is due to OH groups, NH2 or NH stretch (Kim et al., 2007). The sharp peaks seen at 1703 cm−1, 1399 cm−1, and 1193 cm−1 indicate the stretching of CN heterocycles. The presence of sharp peaks belonging to CN confirms the existence of g-C3N4 and BCQD@g-C3N4 (Phang et al., 2022; Sunasee et al., 2019). Additionally, the peak at 1073 cm−1 indicates the CO functional group. The FTIR spectrum of BCQDs is given in Figure S1b. The peak observed at 3369 cm−1 of B-CQDs corresponds to the OH group (Zhang et al., 2021). The peaks at 2945, 1694, 1611, 1397, and 1027 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of B-CQDs correspond to CH, CO, COOH, CO, and B-C groups, respectively (Šafranko et al., 2023; Firoozbakht et al., 2022; Alarfaj et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020; Romanos et al., 2013). Fig. 1c shows the typical XRD diffraction chart of g-C3N4 and BCQD@g-C3N4. The XRD spectrum for BCQD@g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 shows the XRD diffraction peak (002) plane at 27.70° and 27.77°, indicative of aromatic aggregates, respectively (He et al., 2022b; Jin et al., 2022). A slight rightward shift in the XRD peak of g-C3N4 was observed with the addition of B-CQD, which may be due to narrower interlayer and intralayer distances (Phang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). In the XRD spectrum for BCQDs, the diffraction peak corresponding to the (002) amorphous carbon phase was observed at 21.65° (Figure S1c). The results obtained are compatible with the literature (Chaudhary et al., 2016; Monte-Filho et al., 2019). The band gap energies (Eg) of BCQ@g-C3N4s were calculated from the Uv-vis spectrum using Eq. (1) with the Tauc relation. The energy band gap of BCQ@g-C3N4 was found to be 2.76 eV.

After examining the molecular and crystal structure, the structure and morphology of BCQD@g-C3N4 were examined using TEM. As seen in Fig. 2(a-e), it is seen that 2-dimensional and porous structures are formed. In Fig. 2 a, the formation of a graphene-like 2-dimensional layered structure shows that the g-C3N4 structure is formed (Zhang et al., 2020). Fig. 2(b-c) shows the g-C3N4 in the porous structure with different sizes (Li et al., 2023). As seen in Fig. 2(e-d), BCQDs were distributed homogeneously on g-C3N4 plates, without agglomeration. It can be said that BCQDs do not exist freely, but only on the g-C3N4 surface, and play a role in increasing the photocatalytic property (Shi et al., 2018). As seen in Fig. 2f, BCQD is seen to have an average size of 5.1 ± 1.14 nm.

Photocatalytic studies of the synthesized BCQD@g-C3N4 for the degradation of organic dyes under visible light were carried out (Fig. 3 ). Dye degradation results of RhB and MO dyes at different times were determined by monitoring the UV–Vis absorption spectra. As seen in Fig. 3a, the amount of degradation of organic dyes increased depending on time under sunlight. At the end of 120 min, the degradation rates of RhB and MO dyes were found to be 65.58 % and 73.56 %, respectively. MO is among the azo dyes, while RhB is a xanthene dye. Aromatic rings in the MO structure are π-electron acceptors due to their electron-withdrawing sulfuric groups, while RhB is a π-electron donor due to its xanthilium structure (Chen et al., 2019). This structural difference may be effective in the activity of the catalyst. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model calculation was used to determine the photocatalytic activity (Ameen et al., 2023; He et al., 2022b; Girish et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023). Model formulation where k is used as the first-order rate constant;

Fig. 3b shows the time-dependent degradation profile of MO and RhB as a function of BCQD@g-C3N4. In an environment where the photocatalyst and sunlight are kept constant, the amount of dye degradation increases for both dyes. Using BCQD@g-C3N4, the degradation rate kinetics of RhB and MO dyes were calculated by plotting ln(C0/C) versus time. As seen in Fig. 3c, the dye degradation constants k for RhB and MO dyes in the presence of BCQD@g-C3N4 photocatalyst were calculated as 0.009 min−1 and 0.011 min−1, respectively. Reusability studies determined the stability of BCQD@g-C3N4 in photocatalytic reactions and it was observed that they still had high photocatalytic activity against MO and RhB dyes after 4 cycles. Fig. 3d shows the photocatalytic activities of BCQD@g-C3N4 against MO and RhB dyes after 4 cycles and there were decreases in the degradation efficiency of both dyes. This situation may be due to the loss of nanomaterials by centrifugation or washing at the end of each cycle (Ikram et al., 2021). The obtained results show that BCQD@g-C3N4 have a stable structure and are promising for studies on the cleaning of polluted wastewater. The results obtained show that the photocatalyst of BCQD@g-C3N4 provides higher photodegradation for MO. Table 1 shows a comparison of BCQD@g-C3N4s with different catalysts in the literature. Figure S3 shows the photocatalytic activity results of g-C3N4s. The photocatalytic activities of g-C3N4s against RhB and MO dyes were found to be 53.90 % and 61 % at the end of the 120th minute (Figure S3a). Figure S3b shows the time-dependent degradation graph of MO and RhB. Figure S3c shows the ln(C/C0) plot of g-C3N4 versus irradiation time. It was observed that there was a linear relationship between ln(C/C0) and irradiation time, confirming that the photocatalytic reaction was pseudo-first order. Rate constant values for the degradation of dyes were calculated from Figure S3c, and the rate constants for RhB and MO were found to be 0.006 min−1 and 0.007 min−1, respectively. An increase in photocatalytic activity was observed with the addition of BCQDs to g-C3N4s. The fact that BCQDs have better photocatalytic activity than pure g-C3N4s indicates that a successful heterojunction occurs between the two components. The synthesized composite may have increased dye degradation by using visible light better by improving electron and hole recombination rates (Phang et al., 2022; Pham et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2018).

The photocatalytic activity of BCQD@g-C3N4 against E. coli bacteria was examined under sunlight. As seen in Fig. 4 , it is seen that there is a decrease in bacterial colonies depending on the duration of exposure to light. As seen in Fig. 4, it can be seen that bacterial colonies that were not exposed to light initially grew, but after 60 min, the colonies decreased due to the photocatalytic effect of BCQD@g-C3N4, and no colonies were formed after 120 min. The obtained results show that BCQD@g-C3N4 has a photocatalytic activity for the removal of dyes and pathogenic bacteria from wastewater.

The photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, combined with different structures, enabled antibacterial studies to be carried out. In a study conducted in this direction, the antibacterial activity of the AgBr-modified g-C3N4 nanocomposite structure on Pseudomonas putida was tested under light and dark conditions. As a result of the study, it was reported that bright conditions showed higher antibacterial activity (Balapure et al., 2020). In another study, when the antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus was examined using exfoliated g-C3N4, it was observed that there was no bacterial growth after 2 h under light (Attri et al., 2023). In the study conducted by loading g-C3N4 onto polyvinyl alcohol-chitosan nanofiber, it was observed that the bacterial inhibition rate in E. coli was 97 % at the end of 24 h under artificial sunlight (Bai et al., 2023).

The mechanism of photocatalytic activity against bacteria can be estimated that O2 and OH radicals are effective in the photocatalysis reaction, degradation of dyes, and inhibition of E. coli (Sim et al., 2018). With sunlight energy, electrons were excited from the valance band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), and electron-hole pairs were formed on the catalyst. The formation of OH radicals in VB and O2 radicals in CB occurred (Jin et al., 2021). These radicals then attacked the dyes and the bacterial cell, causing MO/RhB degradation and E. coli inactivation (Fig. 5 ).

In this study, the BCQD@g-C3N4 nanocomposite structure was synthesized using the hydrothermal method, and its photocatalytic activity against MO, RhB dyes, and RhB/MO dyes and E. coli bacteria was examined. In order to increase the photocatalytic property, BCQD@g-C3N4 photocatalyst with superior properties was synthesized by hydrothermal route using boron-doped CQD and 2D g-C3N4. The formation of the BCQD@g-C3N4 composite structure was confirmed by XRD, TEM, FL spectrophotometer, and FTIR. In the TEM results, it is seen that BCQDs are distributed on g-C3N4 in a homogeneous structure and the average particle size is found to be 5.1 ± 1.14 nm. In the study conducted on the removal of organic dyes, the photodegradation of RhB and MO was found to be 65.58 % and 73.56 %, respectively. The dye degradation constants k of the BCQD@g-C3N4 photocatalyst for RhB and MO were calculated as 0.009 min−1 and 0.011 min−1, respectively. It was determined that BCQD@g-C3N4s still maintained their photocatalytic activity after 4 cycles. Additionally, the photocatalytic activity of BCQD@g-C3N4 against E. coli bacteria was investigated, and it was observed that the bacterial inhibition was highest at the end of 120 min. As a result, the studies conducted within the scope of this study showed that BCQD@g-C3N4 can be used in the removal of organic dyes and E. coli pathogenic bacteria from wastewater. It is expected in the future that this BCQD@g-C3N4 can be developed to be an efficient photocatalyst against wastewater pollution and show a new approach to overcoming the difficulties of environmental protection studies.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or non-profit sectors.

Aysenur Aygun: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Rima Nour Elhouda Tiri: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Ramazan Bayat: Writing – original draft, Validation. Fatih Sen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
